name: How a loan gets repaid
reg: fixed-rate · interest-only · balloon · the amortization curve

items.text: A fixed-rate loan locks one interest rate for the whole term, so the principal-and-interest payment never changes. Early on, most of each payment is interest; as the balance falls, more of it goes to principal, until the loan is paid off on schedule. Every other product in this unit is a change to that one arrangement — the payment, the payoff date, or both.

On a 30-year loan the crossover — the month principal finally overtakes interest inside each payment — sits more than halfway in. A $200,000 loan at 6% pays $231,676 in interest across the full schedule. Paying extra against principal each month moves the crossover earlier and drops that total sharply.

On an interest-only loan the borrower pays only the interest for a set period, so the balance holds flat — nothing is going to principal. It exists only as a feature of a non-QM mortgage: a lender either keeps it in portfolio or sells it to a private investor. When the interest-only period ends, the loan re-casts to a payment that repays the full balance over the years left, and that payment jumps.

A balloon loan's payment is set on a 30- or 15-year amortization, but the lender calls the entire remaining balance due years earlier. Lenders write it as two numbers — years to the call, then the amortization behind the payment — so a 3/27 is paid like a 30-year loan but comes due at year three. The low rate is the trade for the risk that the borrower can't cover the call. To exercise a conditional right to modify (CRTM) — converting the balloon to a fixed rate instead of paying it — the borrower must give written notice no later than 45 days before the call.

When a borrower pays a lump sum against principal, a fixed-rate loan normally keeps the payment the same and shortens the term. A re-cast does the opposite: the servicer recomputes a lower payment against the new, smaller balance over the remaining term. It's discretionary and often carries a fee — and the interest rate never changes.
